sucibhūtena.meaning: by one become pure; with what has become clean

suddhadhammā.meaning: mere phenomena; nothing but pure (simple, bare) phenomena
breakdown: Pure; here: simple, mere, nothing else but.
suddhadhammā.goenka: The phrase suddhadhammā, “mere phenomena”, expresses the insight that what is conventionally taken as a being or self is, when observed with wisdom, nothing but bare phenomena arising and passing according to conditions — there is no permanent entity behind the process.

suddhamattānaṁ.meaning: (one purifies) oneself well; oneself made pure
suddhamattānaṁ.gram: phrase in sandhi: adjective + reflexive noun (accusative singular)
breakdown: Oneself (accusative of attā). (The -ṁ + a- gives -m- in sandhi.)
suddhamattānaṁ.note: uncertain: the lexicon's running breakdown “su + dhama + attānaṁ — well + blow + oneself” reflects a verse context (sudhantaṁ “well blown”); read here as suddhaṁ + attānaṁ “oneself pure”.

breakdown: Taught, pointed out (past participle of deseti, √dis).

sudesitāni well.note: uncertain: this key is a glossary parsing artifact — the English word “well” from the gloss “well taught” was attached to the headword sudesitāni; see the entry sudesitāni.

suduttaraṁ.meaning: very hard to cross; most difficult to go beyond
breakdown: Root “to cross”: crossing over. Together: very hard to cross.

sugata.meaning: well gone; the Well-Gone One (an epithet of the Buddha)
sugata.goenka: Sugata, “the one well gone”, is one of the standard epithets of the Buddha recited in the formula of recollection: he has gone the good way, walking the path to its end without turning back, and his going is wholly faultless.

sugatavinayā.meaning: from the discipline of the Well-Gone One; the Sugata's code of moral conduct

sugatiṁ.meaning: to a happy state of existence; a good destination (of rebirth)
sugatiṁ.gram: feminine abstract noun (accusative singular), from gati
breakdown: Going, destination, course of rebirth (√gam + ti).

sugato.meaning: well gone; the Well-Gone One (an epithet of the Buddha)

breakdown: Heart (hadaya). Together: of good heart, a true friend.

sukatadukkaṭānaṁ.meaning: of deeds well done and badly done
breakdown: Well done (su + kata, past participle of karoti).

sukhakāmāni.meaning: desiring happiness (of beings that long for happiness)

sukhamanveti.summary: From Dhammapada 2: happiness follows the pure mind as the never-departing shadow.
sukhamanveti.gram: compound verb phrase in sandhi: noun (accusative) + verb 3rd person singular
breakdown: Follows (anu + eti). (The -ṁ + a- gives -m- in sandhi.)

sukhamesāno.gram: present participle phrase in sandhi: noun (accusative) + middle participle
breakdown: Seeking, longing for (middle present participle of esati).

sukhamāvahāti.gram: compound verb phrase in sandhi: noun (accusative) + verb 3rd person singular

sukhino.gram: adjective, from sukha (nominative plural of sukhī)

sukhitattā.meaning: happy in themselves; with gladdened selves
sukhitattā.summary: From the Mettāsutta: sabbe sattā bhavantu sukhitattā — “may all beings be happy in themselves”.
breakdown: Made happy, gladdened (past participle of sukheti).
Self. Together: whose self is happy.

sukkapakkheva.meaning: just as in the bright half (the period of the waxing moon)
sukkapakkheva.gram: masculine noun, compound (locative singular) + emphatic eva

sukkhatiṇakaṭṭhagoma.meaning: dry grass, wood and cow dung

sumedhapatthanākathā, buddhavaṁsapāḷi, k.meaning: “The account of Sumedha's aspiration”, in the Buddhavaṁsa text, of the Khuddakanikāya
breakdown: The account (kathā) of the aspiration (patthanā) of Sumedha, the future Buddha.
The Buddhavaṁsa text, “Chronicle of the Buddhas”.
In the Khuddakanikāya, the Minor Collection (locative).

supaṭipanno.meaning: of good practice; practising well (said of the Saṅgha)
breakdown: Entered upon the path, practising (past participle of paṭipajjati, √pad).
supaṭipanno.goenka: Supaṭipanno opens the traditional recollection of the Saṅgha: supaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho — “the order of disciples of the Blessed One practises well”. The community is worthy of respect not by status but by actual practice: walking the straight path of sīla, samādhi and paññā.

suppatiṭṭhitasaddhe.meaning: whose faith is firmly established; with devotion well grounded
breakdown: Prefix: well. (The doubled pp is sandhi doubling.)
Established, firmly grounded (past participle of patiṭṭhahati).

suppayuttā.meaning: who engage themselves well; well applied (in body, speech and mind)
breakdown: Prefix: well. (The doubled pp is sandhi doubling.)

suppayuttā who engage.note: uncertain: this key is a glossary parsing artifact — the English words “who engage” from the gloss were attached to the headword suppayuttā; see the entry suppayuttā.

breakdown: Prefix: well. (The doubled pp is sandhi doubling.)

supāyāsadhammā.meaning: subject to (sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief and) despair
breakdown: Subject to, having the nature of (at the end of a compound).
supāyāsadhammā.note: uncertain: this key is a line-break fragment of sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsadhammā “beings subject to sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief and despair”.

surādhutto.meaning: a drunkard; one leading a dissolute life through drink
breakdown: Rogue, one of wild dissolute living.

surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānu.meaning: indulgence in intoxicants causing carelessness (truncated form)
surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānu.note: uncertain: this key is a line-break fragment of surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānuyogo; see the full entry.

surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānuyogo.meaning: addiction to liquor, fermented drink and intoxicants, which are occasions for carelessness
breakdown: Intoxicant, what bewilders. (The doubled pp following is sandhi doubling.)
Being addicted to, applying oneself to.
surāmerayamajjappamādaṭṭhānānuyogo.goenka: This long compound names the object of the fifth precept: abstaining from liquor, fermented drink and intoxicants, because they are occasions for carelessness (pamāda). Intoxication clouds sati and sampajañña, the very faculties the meditator works to sharpen, and so opens the door to breaking every other precept.

susamattarūpaṁ.meaning: thoroughly well accomplished; of a quite properly completed kind
breakdown: Form, kind; at the end of a compound: “of such a nature”.

susaṁvihitakammantā.meaning: whose work and duties are well arranged

susaṁvihitā.meaning: well arranged; well disposed, properly organized
breakdown: Arranged, accomplished (past participle of saṁvidahati, saṁ + vi + √dhā).

sussusāya.meaning: by the wish to hear; through willingness to listen, obedience, attendance
sussusāya.gram: feminine abstract noun (instrumental singular), from sussūsati (√su)
breakdown: Desire to hear, willingness to listen (desiderative of √su “to hear”).

susumāra/.meaning: a crocodile (variant listing of susumāra / suṁsumāra)
susumāra/.note: uncertain: this key is a glossary parsing artifact — a trailing slash from the variant listing susumāra/suṁsumāra; see the entry susumāra.

sutadharo.meaning: remembering what has been heard; a keeper of learning
breakdown: What has been heard, learning (past participle of suṇāti).

sutasannicayo.meaning: storing up what has been heard; consolidating one's learning

sutavant puthujjano puggalo.meaning: an instructed ordinary person; an individual worldling who has heard (the teaching)
sutavant puthujjano puggalo.gram: phrase: adjective + masculine noun + masculine noun
breakdown: One who has heard, instructed, learned.
An ordinary worldling, a common person not yet noble.

sutta.meaning: a sutta; discourse of the Buddha (literally: thread, text, line); (as past participle of supati) asleep
sutta.summary: The word sutta is left untranslated: a “thread” of teaching, the discourses collected mainly in the Sutta Piṭaka.
sutta.gram: neuter noun; homonym: pp of supati “asleep”
breakdown: Thread, string, line; hence a text strung together, a discourse.
As a separate word: slept, asleep (past participle of supati).
sutta.goenka: The lexicon notes that the word sutta remains untranslated. Its meaning is “thread, text, line of…”, and it relates in general to the narrational dialogues of the Buddha, mainly collected in the Sutta Piṭaka — the threads of the teaching on which the practice is strung.
